title: Chapter 1: Information Security With HelmetJS - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of HelmetJS middleware and HTTP security headers

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the HelmetJS middleware in an Express application?

options.text: To encrypt all data stored in the database
To set various HTTP response headers that help protect against common web vulnerabilities
To authenticate users with JSON Web Tokens
To compress HTTP responses for faster delivery

questions.explanation: HelmetJS is a collection of middleware functions that set HTTP response headers to help protect Express apps from well-known web vulnerabilities such as XSS, clickjacking, and MIME-type sniffing.

questions.question: Which HTTP header does helmet.hidePoweredBy() remove or modify?

options.text: X-Content-Type-Options
X-Frame-Options
X-Powered-By
X-XSS-Protection

questions.explanation: helmet.hidePoweredBy() removes the X-Powered-By header, which by default reveals that the app is powered by Express. Hiding this header makes it harder for attackers to identify the technology stack and target known vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What does the Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header do?

options.text: It encrypts all content sent to the browser
It specifies which domains are allowed to load resources such as scripts, styles, and images on the page
It forces the browser to use HTTPS for all requests
It prevents cookies from being sent with requests

questions.explanation: The Content-Security-Policy header allows you to define which sources the browser should consider valid for loading resources like scripts, stylesheets, images, and fonts. This is one of the most effective mitigations against Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks.

questions.question: Which HelmetJS function sets the X-Frame-Options header to prevent clickjacking attacks?

options.text: helmet.noSniff()
helmet.xssFilter()
helmet.frameguard()
helmet.hsts()

questions.explanation: helmet.frameguard() sets the X-Frame-Options header, which controls whether the browser is allowed to render a page inside a <frame>, <iframe>, <embed>, or <object> element. This prevents clickjacking attacks where a malicious site embeds your page in an invisible frame.

questions.question: What does HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) enforce?

options.text: It forces the browser to only use HTTPS when communicating with the server for a specified time period
It prevents the browser from caching any responses
It disables all JavaScript execution on the page
It requires HTTP Basic Authentication for every request

questions.explanation: HSTS tells the browser that it should only use HTTPS (never HTTP) to communicate with the server. The Strict-Transport-Security header includes a max-age directive that specifies how long the browser should remember to enforce HTTPS, protecting against protocol downgrade attacks and cookie hijacking.

questions.question: What vulnerability does the X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header prevent?

options.text: SQL injection attacks
MIME-type sniffing, where the browser guesses the content type and may execute malicious files
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
Man-in-the-middle attacks

questions.explanation: The X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header prevents browsers from MIME-type sniffing — the practice of guessing a resource's content type by examining its bytes instead of trusting the Content-Type header. Without this, a browser might execute a malicious file disguised as an innocent file type.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid value for the X-Frame-Options header?

options.text: ALLOW-ALL
DENY
BLOCK
DISABLED

questions.explanation: X-Frame-Options supports three valid directives: DENY (prevents all framing), SAMEORIGIN (allows framing only by the same origin), and ALLOW-FROM uri (allows framing by a specific URI, though this is now deprecated). DENY completely prevents the page from being displayed in any frame.

questions.question: What does helmet.dnsPrefetchControl() do?

options.text: It forces DNS resolution to happen over HTTPS
It controls whether the browser performs DNS prefetching, which can leak information about links on the page
It blocks all DNS queries from the browser
It redirects DNS queries to a secure DNS server

questions.explanation: helmet.dnsPrefetchControl() sets the X-DNS-Prefetch-Control header. Browsers sometimes prefetch DNS records for links on a page to speed up future navigation, but this can reveal to an eavesdropper which external sites appear on the page. Disabling DNS prefetching improves privacy at a small performance cost.

questions.question: How do you install and apply HelmetJS to an Express application?

options.text: npm install helmet, then call app.use(helmet()) to enable all default protections
npm install helmet, then import it and call helmet.start()
npm install express-helmet, then call app.helmet() to activate it
Helmet is built into Express and requires no installation

questions.explanation: HelmetJS is installed via npm install helmet, then applied as middleware using app.use(helmet()). Calling helmet() with no arguments enables a sensible set of default security headers. Individual middleware functions can also be configured separately for finer control.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attack that HelmetJS helps mitigate?

options.text: An attacker intercepts network traffic between the client and server
An attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
An attacker gains direct access to the server's file system
An attacker floods the server with excessive requests to cause a denial of service

questions.explanation: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is an attack where malicious scripts are injected into otherwise trusted web pages. When other users view the page, the injected script executes in their browser, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or other sensitive data. HelmetJS mitigates XSS through headers like Content-Security-Policy and X-XSS-Protection.
